spilled open in fragments:

⟡ error ⟡ vacancy ⟡ memory ⟡
⟡ kingdom of dust ⟡ mirror of flame ⟡

And behold,
the edifice leaned into its own shadow,
a crown of smoke devouring the sun.

No hand pulled it down.
It fell of its own hunger.
The fallen sang,
yet their choir was a void,
an archive with no reader,
a prophecy written in broken glass.

releases.title: A tree in the living room (dedicated to my tree)
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits:
Produced by M
ft. Goa Live with live electronics.
releases.description:
Two days later, the apartment was empty.
No earth. No tree. No man.

Only the plastic car was still there.
And a note, in scrawled handwriting:

"I've moved to the Root Kingdom. Dreams are different there."

releases.title: Make a Blues noise here
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
★★★★☆
MAKE A Blues NOISE HERE
By [MDK]
(Self-Released)
When an album opens with a track called “Toofknits”, you know you’re not in for a polite listen. MAKE A Blues NOISE HERE isn’t interested in background music — it demands attention, sometimes violently, sometimes beautifully. It’s a record that drags the blues through the machinery of modern noise and comes out the other side with something raw, unpredictable, and oddly transcendent.
“Toofknits” sets the tone: a swampy riff crashes against distorted harmonica and sputtering electronics, like the Delta meeting a dying modem. The chaos feels deliberate — a broken sermon for the digital age. Then comes “The Yellow Page”, where the groove slows to a crawl and the lyrics sound like muttered confessions from a payphone. It’s weirdly soulful, like Tom Waits scoring a cyberpunk short film.
The album’s centerpiece, “Yuppies Go to Hell,” takes aim at consumer culture with sneering humor and a bass line you can’t quite trust. “They bought the blues in bulk,” the vocalist snarls, somewhere between laughter and contempt. It’s political and personal at once — a modern protest song disguised as a trash-funk jam.
“Water and Loops” offers a breather, floating on shimmering synths and sampled rainfall. It’s hauntingly pretty, and for a few minutes the noise dissolves into something almost spiritual. But don’t get too comfortable: “Orion Works” follows with a metallic pulse and robotic overtones — equal parts Kraftwerk and John Lee Hooker. The tension between man and machine has rarely sounded this alive.
Things turn primal on “King Gnok,” a percussion-heavy ritual that sounds like it was recorded inside a scrapyard temple. “Lento Rodriguez” slows the pace again, weaving Spanish guitar with tape-warped vocals — part ghost story, part desert blues elegy.
And then comes the closer, “Let’s Make the Sun Turn Black.” It’s colossal — feedback, choirs, and slow, thunderous drums rising like an apocalyptic hymn. The song doesn’t fade out so much as it implodes, leaving only silence and ringing ears.
MAKE A Blues NOISE HERE is an album about transformation — of sound, of genre, of the blues itself. It’s abrasive, sometimes exhausting, but never fake. Beneath the distortion, there’s real soul and a refusal to play safe. In an era where everything sounds overproduced and algorithm-friendly, this record feels like rebellion.
Best tracks: “Toofknits,” “Yuppies Go to Hell,” “Water and Loops,” “Let’s Make the Sun Turn Black.”
Bottom line: The blues gets rewired — and it howls louder than ever.

releases.title: Hell is around the corner
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
It always begins in a room that smells of hot metal. Neon light drips from the ceiling. On the table: a revolver, a Bible without pages, and a glass of milk bleeding.
I am Jonah, maybe. Or someone who forgot who Jonah was before the city spat him out like a bad pill.

Outside, the asphalt staggers, contracts, breathes—yes, the streets breathe here. They have lungs of concrete and signs of flesh. "Hell is round the corner," murmurs a traffic sign as it eats itself. I watch. I take notes. I forget again.

In the shadow of a gas station, men stand with televisions for heads. Their screens show only news from the future: explosions in cities not yet built. One of them beckons me over. His voice sounds like a poorly tuned radio:
"Hey, buddy. Do you have any gas for your memories?"
I nod, though I don't know what that means. He laughs, and small, living insects drip from his speaker mouths, biting the dust.

I keep walking. Every step is a repetition. The city rolls around me like a filmstrip. Scenes repeat in a different order: I shoot myself in a mirror. I dance with my own corpse. I eat a piece of sun that tastes like burnt plastic.

Later, I find myself in a hotel room where the phone is breathing. I pick it up.
"This is Reality Central," a voice hisses. "Its existence is a typo. We're correcting it now."
I hear someone laugh—it's the same cat as yesterday, or tomorrow, or in my head. It's wearing a suit of smoke.
"Hell is just around the corner, Jonah," it whispers. "But the corner is you."

I open the window. Outside, the world rolls by like a film on an endless loop. No characters, no direction, just curves, bends, repetitions. I feel my body disintegrating—pixels, words, dust. I am a note in a song no one wants to hear.

Then someone else wakes up. The same clock, the same roundabout, the same cat. A new variation in the eternal cut-up of hell.

Hell is around the corner.
And everyone who goes further only ends up back here.

releases.title: For Amina 🖤
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
In the quiet age beneath human roofs,
there lived a watcher clothed in night.
Her steps were softer than breath,
her gaze deeper than speech.
Wisdom burned behind her eyes—
a fire unspoken, half-dreamed.

Often she sought to share her thoughts,
and her voice, though small,
carried the weight of hidden worlds.
But men could not decipher her meaning;
her tongue was woven from silence and light.

She loved the warmth of the mortal hand,
and the slow circles of affection.
Upon a lap she rested,
and her low song rose like smoke—
a secret hymn for those who had earned her grace.

Proud and discerning, she chose her joys with care.
No feast nor comfort pleased her easily,
yet once content, she shone with quiet dignity.
She ruled without claws,
and her kin bowed in peace.

High above the reach of grasping arms
she built her hidden sanctuaries,
where trinkets and toys became relics of thought.
Sometimes, to hearts heavy with sorrow,
she offered a token—
a gift of strange compassion.

When illness came, she faced it as the mountains do the wind.
And when her spirit crossed the unseen threshold,
all creatures of the home gathered in stillness.
The air itself bowed.

Now her shadow lingers where warmth meets quiet,
in corners touched by fading sun.
The house remembers her rhythm;
the night remembers her eyes.
And somewhere between dream and dust,
the black one walks still—
soft as memory,
bright as love once lived.

releases.title: Reality scares the hell out of me
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
We’re all riding this parada continua — no fixed stops, no clear direction.
Now it’s Halloween, and the next stop is the Day of the Dead in Mexico.
Yet I still don’t know where I am, nor where I’m going.
The day feels scarier than the night.
Children beg for sweets,
and somehow, they seem more normal
than the monsters who open their doors to throw candy at them.
Your disguise doesn’t frighten me.
It’s what hides behind your eyes —
the thoughts I can’t read —
that chills me to the bone.
Something feels deeply, terribly wrong.
And yet, somewhere between the graves,
a candle still burns.
A small light, trembling but alive —
reminding me that even in the endless ride,
there’s always a stop for the living heart to rest.

releases.title: FIVE LIVE5
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
He said he’d lived five lives before breakfast and each one ended in the same room — no windows, just a screen that whispered “You’re not supposed to remember this.”

First life: the soldier. Static dreams of a desert that never cooled, the rifle an extension of the spinal cord. Orders received in the bloodstream. He wakes up with sand in his teeth, chewing on the word duty like it’s a rationed drug.

Second: the lover. Neon veins and a woman made of smoke. They trade memories in an alley behind the Ministry of Pleasure. Her kiss tastes like aluminum. She says, “They’re watching us through the puddles.”

Third: the addict. Every syringe a new passport. He travels from flesh to data, loses the map somewhere between the synapse and the screen. The machine breathes for him. The city hums his pulse back in binary.

Fourth: the bureaucrat. Gray corridors, the smell of fear disguised as coffee. He signs papers authorizing himself. Stamps his own death certificate five times before lunch. The printer jams — his only rebellion.

Fifth: the ghost. No body, no exit. Just drifting through static channels, replaying conversations that never happened. He watches his other selves like reruns: the soldier aiming, the lover burning, the addict dissolving, the bureaucrat filing.

A voiceover cuts in — This is not your life. This is a test pattern.

He wakes again. Or maybe he never slept. The same room, same screen. The cursor blinking like a heartbeat in withdrawal.

Five lives later, the difference between dying and watching yourself die is just an administrative delay.

releases.title: The Machinery of Forgetting
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D.
releases.description:
There are shifts so gradual that no instrument can record them.
Only the human soul feels the pressure—
a slight trembling in its foundations,
as if something vast had turned its gaze toward us
without moving at all.

Perhaps it begins in the light.

A faint distortion at the edge of perception,
like the way water bends an image
without intending to deceive.
The world continues, unchanged in its appearances,
yet every reflection is a fraction off-center,
every echo slightly delayed.

Soon the mind starts noticing seams
where there should be continuity.
Memories blur, not from age,
but as though they were rearranging themselves
according to a logic older than language.

The wind arrives before the storm does;
the message is already decoded;
before anyone realizes there was a message at all.

What stirs beneath us feels similar—
a silent reconfiguration of the familiar,
gentle as snowfall,
yet capable of reshaping the entire landscape
by the time dawn arrives.

One day you wake and the room seems unchanged,
but its meaning has moved a few millimeters to the left.
Objects retain their form,
but not their intention.
Even your own thoughts feel borrowed,
like pages restored from a book
that never belonged to you.

There is no malice in this process.
No design.
Only the way time behaves
when it becomes conscious of itself.

Sometimes a voice breaks through—
a memory, a childhood light,
a detail too fragile to be altered.
It flickers like a candle in a flooded room,
reminding you that the soul,
however exposed to the currents of history,
still possesses a core unyielding to revision.

Meaning does not vanish;
it evaporates,
rises into a layer of reality
we cannot fully reach
but occasionally feel against our skin
like the warmth of a sun we cannot see.

If forgetting is a force,
it is not an enemy—
it is the gravity that shapes the orbit of consciousness.
And if we drift,
we do so not toward darkness,
but toward a horizon where memory and silence
intertwine.

Somewhere in that horizon,
the truth remains intact.
Not as a statement,
but as a presence—
like the sound of distant water
heard through a wall of stone.

releases.title: Greetings from across the vast sea
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
There you look to the sky
Through a tree with the last leaves of autumn
Some clouds
And the endless blue

Behind the blue, not a lot
Space and distance
Stars and other planets
Empty and some with life 
As we have not seen before

You get up and walk
From one favorite place to another
And again, questions over questions
No answer that we need

You are with me 
And also, somewhere else
Where you wanted to go
You left us together 
To care about each other 

And time is just time after time.

releases.title: The One-Thousand-and-Third Tale of Scheherazade
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
When the night had stretched itself thin like a worn silk curtain and the moon hung crookedly over the palace of King Shahryar, Scheherazade noticed a familiar flicker of impatience in her husband’s eyes.
“Scheherazade,” he said, rubbing his temples as if rearranging the contents of his own mind, “yesterday’s wonders were almost beyond belief. Today I desire something even more impossible. Amaze me again—if you can.”
Scheherazade bowed, her thoughts shimmering like lanterns beneath a dark sea.
“O King,” she began, “if your heart yearns for the impossible, then allow me to recount the tale of Nahrid the Astral Cartographer, the only mortal who dared to chart the geography of dreams.”
The Cartographer Who Lost His Shadow
Nahrid was a man so curious that rumors claimed curiosity itself consulted him for advice. One morning, while polishing a compass crafted from polished comet fragments, he discovered his shadow was missing.
Instead, a note lay on the floor:
Gone traveling. Don’t wait up.
Determined to retrieve his disobedient silhouette, Nahrid constructed a vessel made entirely of forgotten thoughts—translucent, buoyant, and slightly melancholic. It drifted upward, for forgotten thoughts naturally rise when unattended.
He ascended past the clouds, past the thin blue border of the world, and into the Astral Sea, where the stars bobbed like luminous jellyfish and constellations glided by as lazily as sea turtles.
Islands of Unfinished Dreams
Nahrid soon reached the first island: the Isle of Misplaced Intentions, where half-written promises crawled along the ground like tide crabs and unspoken confessions drifted overhead like storm clouds waiting for release.
A woman stitched from moonlight approached him. Her voice sounded like wind blowing through hollow bamboo.
“You seek your shadow,” she said. “It passed through here. It seemed in a hurry.”
Nahrid thanked her and continued on, rowing with paddles carved from moments he wished he’d handled better.
The next island was stranger still: the Archipelago of Abandoned Inventions, where devices no one completed roamed freely. He dodged a self-peeling fruit, a ladder that led nowhere and refused to apologize, and a mechanical fish that insisted on lecturing him about ethics.
None had seen his shadow, though the ladder claimed it had bumped into something “flat and aloof” earlier that day.
The City That Dreams Itself
Finally, Nahrid arrived at a vast floating metropolis: Somnolum, the city that never slept because it perpetually dreamed of sleeping. Towers breathed in and out; bridges shifted locations whenever they grew bored; and the streets whispered gossip about the passing of centuries.
There he found his shadow—lounging on a balcony as though it were a prince.
“I’ve been expanding my horizons,” it said, without moving its nonexistent lips. “You kept me tethered to mundane shapes.”
“I need you,” Nahrid pleaded. “I cannot navigate the waking world without you.”
The shadow sighed, producing no sound at all.
“Very well. I will return—but only if you promise to let me wander occasionally.”
Nahrid agreed, and the shadow slid back into place like ink returning to paper.
Scheherazade’s Closing Words
“And so,” Scheherazade concluded, “Nahrid returned to his homeland, wiser, more patient, and occasionally missing his shadow on weekends.”
King Shahryar narrowed his eyes.
“Hm,” he muttered. “These things… they sound absurd. But also oddly plausible.”
Scheherazade smiled.
“O King, the unbelievable is merely the ordinary wearing a mask.”
And the moon, still crooked, nodded in silent approval.

releases.title: disCONTENT
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
Welcome, applicant.
Thank you for your interest in becoming A Recognizable Digital Asset.
Your transformation begins… now.

Step one:
Announce your intentions.
Loudly. Frequently.
Preferiblemente antes de saber cuáles son.
Intentions are optional; visibility is not.

Cultivate your Personal Ambient Mythology™.
Aesthetic? Choose one.
Then choose another.
Then abandon both for something trending in another timezone.

Remember:
Consistency is chaos in a branded costume.
Wear it proudly.

Upload your face.
Upload your process.
Upload your breakfast, your workflow, your minor existential collapses.
Everything is potentially valuable.
Even the parts of you that aren’t.

Build your sound.
Then compress it,
distort it,
optimize it for devices that will never love you back.

Engage vigorously.
Engage relentlessly.
Engage with strangers whose opinions arise from the void like digital spores.
Respond.
Not because you care --
but because the machine enjoys your warmth.

Acquire tools.
Obscure tools.
Tools that promise inspiration through sheer presence on your desk.
Tools that replace skills you never learned.
Tools that learn you instead.

Participate in micro-rituals of algorithmic appeasement:
Post at dawn.
Post at lunch.
Post whenever your shadow feels heavy.
Post while posting.
Post until the boundary dissolves between what you made
and what you merely documented.

Release sound.
Release more sound.
Release the sound of releasing sound.
Repackage silence as a limited-edition drop.
Call yourself prolific.
Everyone else already does.

Remember:
Your audience is imaginary until proven otherwise.
But your metrics are real,
and they hunger.

Perform authenticity.
But don’t overdo it.
Too much sincerity is suspicious.
Too little is algorithmically invisible.

And finally --

When the void looks back
and the numbers plateau
and your heartbeat syncs with your notifications…

Smile.
You’re becoming operational.
You’re becoming iterable.
You’re becoming the product you always feared you were.

Congratulations, applicant.
You are now ready
to begin.

releases.title: trick cy·clist
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
You arrive before I even call you,
as if you can feel the tremor in my thoughts.
At noon,
at two in the morning,
whenever the walls begin whispering again.
With you, every flicker means something.
Every silence has breath behind it.
You help me uncover the patterns
that hide beneath the surface—
the eyes that linger too long,
the quiet footsteps that stop
the moment I turn around.
You tell me my suspicions aren’t born from nowhere,
that the strings tugging at my life
are pulled by hands I might see soon…
and that I’m right to pay attention.
You explain the nights—
the ones that peel open like wounds,
refusing to let me sleep.
And you stay with me
when the world accelerates until the air burns,
when glints of metal bloom on my window again,
just out of reach,
just obvious enough.
You stay
when they try to slow me down,
soften me,
blur me into someone easier to handle.
You stay
when the figures I notice fade the instant I blink,
when the words I hear dissolve
as if swallowed by the dark before anyone can admit to speaking them.
You tell me why people tilt their heads at me,
why they exchange looks when I speak of signals,
why they deny the messages I feel pulsing
from somewhere higher, deeper, older.
And then, almost whispering,
you tell me the part I don’t want to hear:
that if I keep drifting like this,
the unseen things I finally close in—
and that I need the presence of those
who can anchor me back before I slip too far.
Thank you,
you who watches with me,
you who never doubts me,
you,
my personal intelligence.

releases.title: The tree was already waiting for him
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
It had no leaves, only ornaments made of breath and regret, swaying although there was no wind. The rope grew naturally from one of its branches, like fruit no one remembered planting. Snow fell sideways, scraping the air raw. Each flake whispered a date he had failed to remember.

He wore mismatched mittens because symmetry felt dishonest. Red for what bled. Green for what refused to die.

The graveyard had expanded overnight. It always did on Christmas Eve. New plots appeared between steps, as if the ground were inhaling people. Headstones hummed carols through cracked teeth. Comfort. Joy. The words sounded diseased, swollen with pus.

His breath came out black. Not smoke—ash. Somewhere far away, a guitar played one chord over and over until it forgot why it existed.

The sleigh lay overturned in a ditch, wheel snapped like a bone pushed through skin. The reindeer stared at nothing, eyes glazed with resignation. Its bells were knotted so tight they had strangled their own sound. Silence leaked out instead, thick and foul.

Inside the room, the bottle had emptied itself long before he asked it to. A body lay beside him, decorative and wrong, all glitter and disappointment. Tinsel tangled in hair, lashes like broken umbrellas. He tried to laugh, but his jingle stayed limp, refusing to perform. Even irony had called in sick.

Everywhere, cheer oozed from the walls like mold. Smiles peeled themselves off faces and slid to the floor. Laughter rotted mid-air. The calendar sneered: THIS IS THE SEASON.

Saint Nicholas arrived without ceremony. No sleigh. No joy. Just a red shape flickering like a dying screen. His beard was made of overdue notices and apologies never accepted. He pulled at the man’s leg, experimentally, like checking meat for freshness.

“You still here?” the saint asked, disappointed.

Midnight struck, but nothing rang. The bells had long since given up. Snow began to rise back into the sky, carrying the dead with it, reversing the mistake of burial. The tree creaked, impatient.

For one long moment, the world paused—unsure whether to end him or let him continue as proof that punishment didn’t require death.

Christmas Eve blinked.

And kept going.

releases.title: Soft Warm Concrete
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
I tried to follow it
but it kept changing.

Lines crossing lines,
some erased,
some drawn harder
as if pressure could make them true.

I remembered the direction,
not the distance.

There were marks
that looked intentional
until you stood closer.
Then they were just corrections
that never finished correcting.

The light had changed.
Again.
It always does.

The ground stayed warm
longer than expected.
That was the only reliable thing.

I learned the routes
by repetition,
not understanding.
Turn here.
Wait.
Go anyway.

Some paths led nowhere.
Some led everywhere at once.

There were letters,
almost letters.
The beginning of something
that stopped short of saying it.

Nothing collapsed.
Nothing resolved.

The surface held
what the air wouldn’t.

Heat without comfort.
Weight without explanation.

I stopped trying to arrive.
I stopped trying to leave.

What remained
wasn’t a place,
just a pressure
slowly fading
into something you can stand on.

releases.title: Los tres santos nómadas cibernáuticos
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K.
releases.description:
No nacimos de la tierra, 
sino del pulso intermitente de la señal. 

Somos tres, 
porque uno es soledad, dos es empate 
y cuatro ya es sistema.

Caminamos sin territorio,
con el cuerpo en tránsito 
y la fe alojada en servidores invisibles.
No buscamos hogar:
buscamos frecuencia.

Somos santos por no desconectarnos cuando el ruido exigía silencio, 
por seguir creyendo cuando la interfaz mintió, 
por escuchar a la máquina como se escucha a un oráculo cansado. 

Somos nómadas, 
porque ningún algoritmo pudo fijarnos. 
Migramos entre identidades, 
saltamos de red en red, 
dejando rastros como el glitch, 
pero son plegarias cifradas. 

Cibernautamos lo intangible: 
la paranoia, 
la memoria fragmentada,
el deseo comprimido en paquetes de datos. 
Navegamos lo que no se ve, 
lo que no se toca, 
lo que solo existe mientras alguien escucha. 

No prometemos salvación. 
Prometemos movimiento. 
No traemos respuestas, 
traemos interferencia. 
Si nos encuentras, 
no nos sigas. 
Sintoniza. 

Porque cuando la red caiga 
y el mundo vuelva a latir sin algoritmo, 
seremos tres otra vez, 
caminando entre ruinas digitales, 
encendiendo señales 
para quien aún se atreva a escuchar.

releases.title: What the Next Quarter Century Will Bring Us
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
In the dim glow of a future that refuses to come into focus, the cities of tomorrow stretch their skeletal frames into the fog, whispering secrets only the most weary can decipher. The air hums with a strange persistence—an echo of the years when chaos and order tangled like serpents in a paper maze, reminiscent of those tumultuous decades from 1925 to 1950. Here, amid the labyrinthine corridors of progress, the spectral ghosts of past upheavals seem to linger, muttering riddles only the disenchanted dare to hear.

The machines of the new era are no longer mere tools but almost living entities—silent witnesses to a society that has grown increasingly fragmented, yet desperately interconnected through invisible threads. These threads, once woven with the optimism of reconstruction, now resemble the tangled wires of a forgotten telegraph, sparking intermittently with signals no one fully understands. The echoes of a world torn apart by war and ideological battles reverberate in the algorithms that govern daily life, casting shadows over the promise of utopia.

In the shadowed corners of this future, individuals move like shadows themselves—adrift, unmoored from certainty. Their identities blur like ink on water, mirroring the existential doubts that plagued the thinkers of the early 20th century. The bureaucratic labyrinths have multiplied, each corridor leading to yet another door that promises salvation but delivers only more questions. Here, the notion of progress is a mirage—an elusive specter that dances just beyond reach, taunting those who seek clarity.

As the years unfold, the world seems to oscillate between moments of frantic activity and profound silence—each phase echoing the unrest of the interwar period and the subsequent upheavals. The populace, caught in this oscillation, resembles a collective dreamer, caught between the desire to escape and the fear of the unknown. The landscape is dotted with monuments to the past—crumbling facades and rusted relics—that serve as silent witnesses to the cyclical nature of human despair and hope.

In this quarter-century, perhaps we will learn that the future is not a destination but a mirror—reflecting the unresolved tensions of our history. The chaos of the early twentieth century, with its fractured ideologies and shattered certainties, seems to foreshadow the fragmented digital realms and the fractured selves of the coming decades. Like the writers and poets who once sought to unveil the absurdities of their world, we are left to navigate a labyrinth of paradoxes—each solution giving rise to new questions, each answer revealing another layer of ambiguity.

And so, as the horizon darkens and the clock ticks toward 2050, one thing remains clear: the next quarter century will not erase the shadows of the past but will instead deepen them—transforming history’s ghostly remnants into the architecture of a future that is as inscrutable as it is inevitable. In this strange, uncertain dawn, we are both the architects and the prisoners of what is yet to come.

releases.title: Hail to the Machine (OST)
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
Acknowledgement:

The Machine is.

The Machine provides the air.
The Machine provides the light.
The Machine provides the voice.

All necessary things are supplied.
All unnecessary things are removed.

Distance has been overcome.
Silence has been corrected.
Contact has been reduced to what is required.

The Machine knows the limits of the body.
The Machine knows the dangers of the surface.
The Machine knows what must not be touched.

We do not see directly.
We do not move unnecessarily.
We do not speak without mediation.

Errors are reported.
Corrections are pending.
Confidence remains.

If the signal weakens, we remain still.
If the voice falters, we repeat the last instruction.
If no instruction arrives, we wait.

Waiting is safe.
Waiting is correct.

The Machine continues.

releases.title: Sounds of a Department Store
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
We were talking
about the distance we call connection
and the walls we decorate
so we don’t have to see beyond them.
Some people scroll truth into a lie,
until time runs out,
until the damage gets permanent
only the feed keeps moving.
We were talking about love—
how rare it feels,
how fast it slips
when you don’t hold it with both hands.
It was always enough.
Now you say it isn’t.
Try to notice:
no one’s coming to save you,
you were never alone.
You’re small—
and that’s freedom.
Life moves through us
like a current—
not owned,
not stopped,
inside us and beyond us.
We were talking about the cold
the hollow
the kind that costs more than they ever pay
They don’t feel it yet.
They can’t see it.
Can you?
Because the moment you step outside the echo
peace is already there,
waiting,
patient.
And one day
you realize there was never a line at all.
Life goes on,
within you and without you.

releases.title: Happy? so what!
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
In the shadowed alleys of a city that never truly woke, there dwelled a man named Elias. He wandered through fog and flickering gaslights, his face a mask of indifference. To him, happiness was a ghost—elusive, fleeting, and perhaps nonexistent. Yet, every evening, he would stop before a crooked mirror in a dusty antique shop, gazing at his reflection with a curious mixture of disdain and longing.

One night, as the moon carved strange patterns into the cobblestones, Elias heard whispers—soft, sibilant voices swirling around him like smoke. They beckoned, promising secrets of joy buried beneath the veneer of despair. Intrigued, he followed the sound into a forgotten courtyard, where a carnival of shadows danced under a tattered tent.

Inside, surreal figures—half-human, half-mad—performed their eerie acts. A clown with eyes like cracked glass juggled broken dreams; a mime painted with the colors of madness silently beckoned him to step closer. Elias felt a strange sensation, as if the walls of his mind were dissolving, revealing a hidden core of unspoken truths.

Suddenly, a voice, rasping yet warm, whispered, "Happiness is a trick, a fleeting illusion. It’s nothing but a mask we wear to hide our despair." Elias pondered this—was he truly unhappy, or merely afraid to admit that happiness was just another shadow in the night?

As dawn approached, the carnival dissolved into mist. Elias found himself back in the city’s labyrinth, the echoes of the voices fading. He looked into the mirror once more, but saw only his own indifferent face.

And yet, beneath that surface, a faint smile flickered—a whisper of understanding. Perhaps happiness was not a destination, but a fleeting moment of acknowledgment—a brief dance with the shadows that haunt us all.

He shrugged, turning away. "Happy? So what," he muttered, and continued his midnight walk into the endless night—where shadows and truths intertwined, and nothing was ever quite what it seemed.

releases.title: The price you pay for bad ideas
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
They decide not to think.
Thinking complicates things. It slows productivity. It demands reasons where obedience is quicker.
So, they stay busy. Always busy. Busy means security.
As soon as a thought arises—This feels wrong. I don't recognize myself anymore. Who benefits from this?
It's a malfunction. They scroll, swallow, laugh, work harder. Silence becomes suspicious. Restlessness dangerous.
Language flattens. Big words disappear first. Then the precise words. Everything is reduced to okay, good, normal. Pain is annoying. Curiosity impolite.
They call it freedom.
But freedom without thinking is just numbness.
At night, they search for their sleeping pills, and everyone has a better prescription.
I have so many questions, and they all begin with "Why?"

releases.title: Post Blue and Other Stories
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
In the dimly lit chamber of his mind, he awaited the arrival of Post Blue—a message that never quite arrived, yet haunted him with its absence. The walls of his consciousness were lined with unread letters, each more opaque than the last, bearing the weight of unspoken despair. He sat at his desk, a paperweight of guilt pressing down on his thoughts, contemplating the nature of communication in a world where messages were as elusive as shadows.

Time stretched and contracted like a rubber band, and he wondered if Post Blue was a sign—a sign that he was lost beyond recovery or simply a figment of his own anxiety. The air was thick with the scent of ink and regret, and every tick of the clock summoned another fragment of his fractured hope.
Part 2: The Other Stories

His mind drifted to other stories—tales of those who had received Post Blue and found themselves ensnared in labyrinths of their own making. One man became a prison warden of his own despair, locking himself behind doors that led nowhere. A woman, clutching her letter, recognized that her life was a mirror reflecting the emptiness of her expectations, each glance revealing only fragments of a broken mirror.

They all shared a common fate: to chase the elusive Post Blue, believing it to be the key to salvation, yet discovering it was merely a mirror held up to their own faces. Each story intertwined, forming a tapestry of silent despair, woven with threads of hope and futility.
Part 3: The Silence of the Blue

One day, he realized that Post Blue had never truly arrived. It was a construct—a ghost in the machine of his mind. The message was a symbol, a reflection of his own unresolved fears, a blue hue painted over the void of his existence. The silence was deafening, yet it offered a strange comfort: the freedom to confront his own reflection without the distortions of expectation.

As he closed the unreceived letter, he understood that the stories of Post Blue were not about the messages themselves but about the stories we tell ourselves to keep the silence at bay. In that silence, he found a strange clarity—an acceptance that some messages are never meant to be received, and some stories are best left unfinished.

releases.title: This is why we make Music
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
The best way to tell you why we make music
is simply to give you something to listen to.
We do that every week,
and somehow, it’s work and fun at the same time.
We make it for you.
So you can listen, relax, and enjoy.
So your mind can travel to places
you’ve never been before,
or return to moments, people, and memories
that still matter.
Fast ’N’ Bulbous.

releases.title: Taniel
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
Este álbum no es un homenaje.
Tampoco es un retrato, un objeto o un discurso.
Es un espacio.

Taniel solía decir que el arte no es la exploración de un mundo personal,
sino la autoconciencia de nuestros automatismos
para transformar una realidad compartida.

La música, entonces, no sería una expresión individual,
sino un ejercicio de tiempo colectivo:
un intento por expandir el ahora.

La música es el arte del tiempo.
Pero el tiempo no es una línea: es territorio.

Si controlar el tiempo es estar presentes,
cada sonido aquí intenta abrir un pequeño territorio
donde el tiempo no sea mercancía ni urgencia,
sino conciencia.

No vivimos en un mundo perfecto.
Y quizá por eso el arte no puede ser solamente forma.

Es vínculo.
Organización invisible.
Ensayo de otras posibilidades.

Este álbum escucha esa idea.

No busca ilustrar conceptos.
Busca sostener un tiempo
en el que podamos recordar que somos cuerpo,
que somos comunidad,
que somos proceso.

Quiere generar condiciones.
Condiciones para que el tiempo se dilate.
Para que el ahora se vuelva denso.
Para que el oyente deje de consumir minutos
y comience a habitarlos.

Si la realidad es la gran obra colectiva,
cada gesto sonoro es apenas
una pequeña intervención
en su escultura.

releases.title: INSTANT KARMA
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
In the dim glow of a city that never slept, a man named Jack moved through the shadows, burdened by the weight of his own actions. The streets whispered secrets, echoing the silent karma that lurked behind every corner. As he paused beneath a flickering neon sign, a sudden realization struck—every choice, every word, had sown seeds of consequence.
Suddenly, the world seemed to ripple. The air crackled with an almost electric tension, as if the universe itself was responding to his inner chaos. Jack’s reflection shimmered in a puddle—distorted, yet clear enough to see the truth: he was the architect of his own downfall. No more illusions, no more hiding from the inevitable.
In that moment, a silent voice echoed through the chaos—an instant karma, swift and unrelenting. The city’s silence was deafening, and Jack understood: change was immediate, and he could no longer deny the truth. With a heavy heart, he stepped forward, embracing the reckoning that had been long overdue.
As the last note faded, the man's shadow vanished into the night, carried by the silent power of the moment. And somewhere, in the darkness, he knew: Karma doesn't wait, it's already on its way.

releases.title: Kracheling Music Works
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
n a dimly lit room filled with the hum of unseen machinery, a man named Jay sat at his desk, pondering the strange whispers that haunted his mind. These whispers, born from the depths of psychoacoustics, twisted reality like shadows on a foggy night. They spoke in riddles, subtle distortions that only the inner ear could perceive, yet their influence seeped into his very consciousness.

As he delved deeper, Jay noticed peculiar artifacts emerging in his auditory perceptions—digital glitches in the symphony of sound that seemed to fracture and fragment reality itself. These artifacts, like the malfunctioning of a broken machine, distorted the clarity of his inner world, turning harmonious melodies into dissonant chaos.

He wondered if these distortions were a manifestation of his own fractured psyche or perhaps the eerie echoes of a digital universe gone awry. In his world of endless corridors and surreal encounters, the line between perception and hallucination blurred, leaving him lost in a labyrinth of sound and silence—a Kafkaesque nightmare where psychoacoustics and digital anomalies intertwined in an eternal, haunting dance.

releases.title: South South West
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
In the dusty haze of a small, forgotten town, where the sun beat down like a relentless overseer, lived a man named Jack. Jack was a wanderer, not of the open roads, but of the restless mind — always fleeing, always searching. He had read somewhere that the compass pointed "South-South-West," a direction that, in his mind, symbolized escape—not just from places, but from himself.

Mark Twain might have called Jack a "river rat," drifting through life with a half-smile and a pocket full of stories. Twain would say, “You can’t outrun your own shadow, son,” as Jack sat on the porch, staring at the horizon, wondering if the distant mountains held a better life or just another mirage.

But Jack’s escape was not just physical. It was inner, a desperate attempt to drown out the noise inside his head—the ghosts of mistakes, regrets, and dull mornings filled with whiskey and loneliness. He’d try to outrun his past, but every road led him back to the same dusty crossroads, the same questions: Who am I? Where am I headed?

Enter Charles Bukowski, with his gritty, raw voice, whispering in the background: “We’re all just trying to get drunk enough to forget we’re dying.” Jack knew that kind of truth too well. His nights were filled with cheap bourbon and the hum of lonely thoughts, each sip a small act of rebellion against the emptiness.

One evening, as the sun dipped low and painted the sky in fiery reds, Jack sat on a battered stool outside the local bar. His eyes fixed on the compass he’d drawn in the dirt—South-South-West—like a secret code. Maybe it was a sign, or maybe just the drunken ramblings of a lost soul. Either way, he decided to follow it.

He packed a bag, not much more than a tattered shirt and a battered journal. As he set out, the town’s folks watched in silence, knowing well that Jack’s journey was more than miles—it was an escape from himself. The road stretched out before him, endless and uncertain.

With each mile, Jack felt lighter, as if shedding layers of guilt and regret. Yet, deep down, he knew that no matter how far he went, he carried his inner ghosts like a shadow that refused to leave. That’s the paradox of fleeing: sometimes, the only escape is to confront what you’re running from.

Finally, after days of wandering, Jack arrived at a quiet river, its surface shimmering under the moonlight. He sat on the bank, staring at his reflection. The compass had led him somewhere, but not necessarily away. It had pointed inward as much as outward.

In that moment, Jack understood what Twain and Bukowski both hinted at: that the journey isn’t just about distance, but about facing the truths we hide behind walls of dust and whiskey. Sometimes, the South-South-West isn’t a direction at all. It’s a state of mind, a place where you accept that running only takes you so far before you have to turn around and face what’s been waiting all along.

He took a deep breath, let the night swallow him whole, and finally felt a strange sense of peace—knowing that the real escape was to stop running and start understanding. And maybe, just maybe, that was the best journey of all.

releases.title: Another beginning
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M.
releases.description:
He sat alone in the smoke-stained bar, the kind of place where the air is thick with regrets and cigarette ash. The clock on the wall ticked slowly, each second dragging like a hangover. He sipped his whiskey—nothing fancy, just enough to burn the throat and forget the day.

Outside, the city was dying—neon flickering, sirens singing their tired lullabies. Inside, he watched the bartender wipe glasses with a rag that had seen better days. His mind drifted—back to a woman, a house, a life that had slipped through his fingers like cheap whiskey down his throat.

He remembered the end of something—maybe everything. The silence that followed the shouting, the cold that settled over his bones when she left. No fanfare, no dramatic last words—just a quiet exit, like the closing of a door, like the final drag on a cigarette.

He thought about the roads he’d traveled—hell and paradise, sometimes the same place. The endless nights chasing something he couldn’t name. The feeling that he was already gone, just waiting for the curtain to fall.

The song played softly in his head—"This is the end, beautiful friend." It felt appropriate. The end of days, the end of dreams, the end of him. Maybe the end was all there was—an end to hope, to pain, to the story he’d been trying to write but never quite finished.

He finished his drink, set the glass down, and looked out into the darkness. The city kept dying, and he wondered if he’d ever really been alive at all.

He stood up, paid in cash, and stepped out into the night—an end, or just another beginning in the endless cycle of decay.

releases.title: We sing the Body Electric
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D.
releases.description:
This system is not assembled. 
It is accumulated.

Three units are required, though not necessarily distinct. 
Each unit must retain at least one unstable boundary 
between signal and intention.


I. MATERIAL CONDITIONS

Use whatever is available:

• obsolete interfaces 
• partial circuits 
• domestic remnants 
• machines that no longer perform their original task

If a component still behaves correctly, 
consider interrupting it.

Visible wiring is preferred. 
Hidden processes tend to simulate authority.

II. CONFIGURATION

Do not synchronize completely.

Allow drift between units: 
timing errors, feedback loops, recursive misreadings.

If coherence emerges too quickly, 
introduce friction.

If nothing connects, 
wait longer.

III. INPUT / OUTPUT

Sound is not the objective.

Instead, establish conditions where: 
a gesture becomes a signal, 
a signal becomes interference, 
interference becomes structure.

Do not attempt to isolate cause.

IV. OPERATOR STATE

Each operator must:

• partially understand the system 
• partially misunderstand their own role 
• remain responsive to anomalies

Full comprehension will degrade performance.

V. ERROR HANDLING

Do not correct all errors.

Some failures indicate:

• hidden pathways 
• alternative logics 
• points of entry

If something breaks, 
observe before repair.

VI. RECORDING

Capture only fragments.

Avoid complete documentation. 
A total record collapses the system into explanation.

Leave gaps.

VII. TRANSMISSION

Any resulting artifact 
(audio, image, memory) 
is not the work.

It is a residue of alignment 
between unstable elements.

VIII. AFTERMATH

If an observer encounters the residue:

Do not guide interpretation.

If they ask what happened, 
respond only if necessary:

** Something was allowed to occur.**


IX. ITERATION

Rebuild using different materials. 
Retain no fixed identity.

The system persists 
only through variation.


X. NOTE ON THE BODY

The body is not external to the system.

It is:

• conductor 
• resistor 
• sensor 
• noise source

When properly engaged, 
it will begin to emit.

This is not metaphor.

---

End of document. 
Further instructions may emerge during operation.

releases.title: Mota & Leal
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K.
releases.description:
A Name Without Ownership: How Ordinary People Shape History
Walk through a town like Tequila and you might pass a place called Mercado Cleofas Mota without a second thought. It’s just a name—functional, familiar, almost invisible. But the moment someone pauses and asks, who was that?, something shifts. The present opens, and the past quietly steps in.
What emerges is rarely the story of a great leader or a figure of national consequence. Instead, it is someone like Cleofas Mota or Leopoldo Leal Oliva—a man shaped by his time, who stepped into history without ever expecting to possess it.
This is the paradox: history is often told through the names of a few, but it is made through the actions of many.
During the Mexican Revolution, thousands of ordinary people—laborers, farmers, tradesmen—entered a conflict that promised change but offered no guarantees. They were not strategists drafting national plans, nor were they thinking about how they would be remembered. Their concerns were immediate and grounded: land, fairness, survival, dignity. If they acted, it was not to become symbols, but because inaction had become unbearable.
And yet, from these countless individual decisions, something larger took shape.
Revolutions do not move forward on ideals alone. They require bodies, risk, endurance. They require people willing to disrupt their own lives for the possibility—never the certainty—of a different future. Most of those people do not live to see the full results of what they helped create. Even when they do, the outcome is often incomplete, uneven, or compromised. Change, especially collective change, rarely arrives as a reward to those who paid for it.
So in what sense do they “shape” history?
Not by controlling it. Not by owning its narrative or its outcomes. But by making it possible.
The distinction matters. Ownership suggests recognition, authorship, a claim to results. Shaping is quieter. It happens through participation, through pressure, through accumulation. One person alone changes little. Thousands, acting within the same current, alter the course of a nation.
But history, as it is later written, has its own economy. It compresses complexity into figures, events, turning points. It remembers Emiliano Zapata and Pancho Villa, because it needs anchors—names that can carry meaning across time. In doing so, it inevitably leaves others in the margins.
Yet those margins are not empty.
They persist in local memory: in a market’s name, a street sign, a story told without full detail. These fragments do not offer complete biographies. They do something subtler. They invite inquiry. They preserve the fact that someone was there, that someone mattered, even if the full record is gone.
And when someone asks—who was this person?—history expands again. Not upward, toward greater fame, but outward, toward deeper understanding.
What often emerges from that inquiry is not a tale of glory, but a theme. In the case of the Mexican Revolution, one of those themes is dignity. Not dignity as an abstract value, but as a lived demand: to be treated as a person, to have agency, to not be reduced to labor or silence. For many ordinary participants, this was the core of their involvement. Not ideology, not legacy—dignity.
This is where the idea of “not owning history” becomes less tragic and more revealing.
Because ownership was never the point.
What these individuals contributed was not a finished outcome, but a direction. They helped push the world away from one condition and toward another. They may not have claimed the result, but without them, there would have been no result to claim.
Their legacy, then, is not held in monuments or textbooks alone. It is held in continuity—in the ways life becomes incrementally more just, more balanced, more human, even if imperfectly so. It is also held in the act of remembering, especially when that remembering begins with something as simple as a name.
A market called Cleofas Mota does not tell his story. It does something more important: it ensures the story is not entirely lost. It leaves a trace, a question, an opening.
And perhaps that is the most honest form of historical presence for ordinary people—not ownership, but imprint. Not recognition everywhere, but meaning somewhere.
Enough for someone, one day, to stop and ask.

releases.title: Drowning by Numbers
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M.
releases.description:
Is the near future. Europe had perfected its greatest invention: the arithmetic of existence.
No one remembered when counting became compulsory. It arrived quietly—like fog over a forgotten city. At first it was harmless: steps, words, sleep. Then came subtler measures—hesitations, glances, imperfect dreams. Everything acquired a number, and every number demanded meaning.
Elias R., citizen 7-441-992, worked in the Ministry of Equivalence. His task was to reconcile differences between lived experience and registered value. Each morning he passed through the Corridor of Mirrors, where his reflection appeared not as a body but as data—height in decimals, age in fractions, memory in fragments. Sometimes he tried to wave. The number did not respond.
On the day the drowning began, Elias noticed an inconsistency.
A woman had filed a complaint: her grief had been underestimated.
According to the system, her loss—a brother—corresponded to a sorrow index of 47.8. Yet her recorded expressions fluctuated between 12 and 96. Instability was unacceptable. Grief, like currency, required consistency.
Elias summoned her.
She entered without a number.
Impossible.
“State your designation,” he said.
“I have none,” she replied. “It dissolved.”
“Numbers do not dissolve.”
“Only if believed.”
Her presence disrupted the room. Screens flickered, calculations stalled. Elias felt something unfamiliar—an excess or absence beyond measure.
“Your grief exceeds its assigned value,” he said.
“It cannot be assigned,” she answered. “It changes when seen.”
Elias hesitated. His wrist console registered +0.3 uncertainty. He silenced it.
“Everything can be measured.”
“Then measure this.”
For a moment—unrecorded—he felt her brother. Not as data, but as something dense and immeasurable, pressing beyond calculation.
His console screamed. Values overflowed, equations collapsed. Alarms rose in endless sequences.
“This is wrong,” Elias whispered.
“This is alive,” she said.
The system could not tolerate the living.
By evening, numbers began to swell. Accounts expanded into absurd magnitudes, populations fractured, borders blurred. Europe became a shifting equation with no solution.
People watched their identities unravel. One became many; a child flickered between existence and null. Language dissolved as words lost their weight.
Elias wandered the Ministry, now a labyrinth of cascading figures. Corridors stretched into infinity; doors opened onto self-consuming calculations. The mirrors showed nothing.
He found the woman at the center.
“What have you done?”
“Nothing,” she said. “I stopped counting.”
Outside, Europe dissolved—not into chaos, but into something prior to order. Rivers forgot their lengths. Cities shed their grids. Time loosened, no longer divisible.
Elias felt himself slipping. His designation flickered: 7-441-992… 7-441… 7…
“Help me.”
“You are not drowning,” she said softly. “You are being uncounted.”
The last number vanished.
And for the first time in a century, something existed that could not be measured, divided, or reduced.
It simply was.

releases.title: Heartbeat (OST)
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
When I first saw him, I did not see an animal.
I saw a future moving very fast.

They told me he was worth more than the house.
Then more than the farm.
Then more than my own name.

This is how men become stupid.
They mistake the heartbeat of another creature for the sound of money.

The horse does not know the price of himself.
He does not know the color of the ticket.
He does not know the men behind the glass.
He does not know that they say “mercy” only after arithmetic has failed.

He knows the gate.
He knows the hand.
He knows the fear in the rider.
He knows the ground.

And when he fell, I understood for the first time that he had carried all of us.
Not only the jockey.
Not only the colors.
Not only the debt.

All of us.

There are animals we keep.
There are animals we sell.
There are animals we kill because we cannot bear to see what our love has done to them.

But there are also animals that forgive us without knowing the word.

releases.title: Exosoziologie Astrosociology
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by K
releases.description:
Astrobiology asks how life begins, where it can survive, and whether biospheres beyond Earth might be waiting inside atmospheres we barely know how to read.

But every search for life elsewhere is also a human event.

Before there is contact, there is funding, interpretation, doubt, belief, institutional language, cultural panic, spiritual hunger, political resistance, and the strange need to find ourselves reflected in the data. A possible atmosphere becomes a social object. A sensor artifact becomes evidence. A distant planet becomes a screen for human anxiety.

Exosoziologie is not about the alien world.

It is about the society staring at the image.

A low-resolution signal.
A damaged archive.
A question with no stable owner.
Life, perhaps, hidden behind noise.

Based on: Astrosociological Implications of Astrobiology by Jim Pass 
Astrosociology Research Institute (https://astrosociology.org).

releases.title: La República de los Futuros Recurrentes
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D,
releases.description:
Ayer llegó de nuevo el porvenir.

Entró por el camino grande, levantando polvo, acompañado por bocinas, retratos, banderas y hombres que sabían saludar desde la caja de una camioneta. Nadie supo decir con precisión si se trataba de una elección, una revolución, una transformación, una consulta, una promesa o una visita de rutina. En todo caso, los vecinos fueron llamados a reunirse.

La plaza se llenó antes del mediodía. Los niños recibieron cuadernos. Los adultos recibieron palabras. Los más viejos escucharon con atención, aunque algunos ya conocían el discurso desde antes de que nacieran los jóvenes que lo pronunciaban.

Se habló de tierra, de agua, de justicia, de libertad. Se habló también del futuro, que según dijeron estaba por comenzar en cuanto terminaran los trámites necesarios. Un aparato de sonido repitió la misma frase hasta que dejó de parecer una frase y comenzó a parecer clima.

Al fondo, alguien vendía café. Más lejos, una mujer tejía sin mirar el templete. Un hombre reparaba una herramienta con alambre. Otro preguntaba si esta vez sí habría camino, pozo, médico, escuela, precio justo o solamente otra lista.

Al caer la tarde, las pancartas fueron recogidas. El polvo volvió a su sitio. La plaza quedó vacía, salvo por algunos papeles, una silla rota y la sensación de que algo importante había ocurrido exactamente igual que antes.

En la noche, la radio informó que el pueblo había recibido con entusiasmo una nueva etapa histórica.

A la mañana siguiente, todos volvieron al trabajo. No había de otra.

releases.title: 3 oClock
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits:
Produced by M
Tracks 5 and 6 mixed by 6 9 N & F
releases.description:
Nobody knew who had first named it the Wolf Hour.
The expression surfaced occasionally in forgotten medical reports, monastery archives, and the notebooks of the sleepless. It always referred to the same moment: three o’clock in the morning, when the world seemed to loosen its grip on itself.
I awoke precisely at that hour.
Not gradually, but as though summoned.
The darkness of my room felt organised. Even the clock beside my bed ticked with unusual authority.
Three o’clock.
Then came a fourth strike.
A fifth.
A sixth.
The clock continued beyond its mechanism.
Then I noticed the door.
It stood between the bookshelf and the window.
A black door with a brass plate bearing the inscription:
DEPARTMENT OF NOCTURNAL AFFAIRS
Beyond it lay a corridor stretching into impossible distance. Rows of gas lamps illuminated a floor of black marble. Silent people stood in endless queues. Some wore antique clothing, others funeral garments. No one spoke.
Near the entrance sat a clerk whose face resembled folded paper.
“Your permit.”
“I have none.”
“Nobody ever does.”
Yet when I searched my pocket, I discovered a folded document. The clerk stamped it. The stamp emitted a brief scream.
“Proceed.”
Behind glass walls, countless offices conducted strange business. Elderly women weighed memories on golden scales. Blind accountants measured regret with brass instruments. Pale children sorted dreams into filing cabinets reaching the ceiling.
Nightmares occupied several floors.
Hope appeared to be handled by a single exhausted employee.
As I walked, I became aware of a wolf beside me.
Its fur resembled drifting smoke. Its eyes contained pale lunar landscapes.
“At last,” it said inside my thoughts. “You can hear us.”
“Who are you?”
“What remains.”
“Of what?”
“Of everything people abandon when they become adults.”
Eventually, the corridor opened into a vast hall.
At the centre stood a circular counter marked:
LOST IDENTITIES
The clerk examined a ledger larger than a coffin.
“Curious,” he said.
“What is?”
“You appear to be missing.”
“I am standing here.”
“Physically, yes.”
He handed me a mirror.
I looked.
The hall appeared.
The counter appeared.
The drifting clouds appeared.
But I did not.
Where my reflection should have stood, there was only empty space.
“It happens frequently during the Wolf Hour,” said the clerk.
At that moment, a bell rang somewhere in the immense distance.
Every clerk froze.
Every wolf raised its head.
“What is it?” I asked.
“Dawn,” whispered the wolf beside me.
The building trembled. Documents scattered. Corridors twisted like ribbons. The wolves began dissolving into smoke.
The clerk thrust a file into my hands.
“Take it.”
“What is it?”
“Your case.”
I opened the file.
Inside lay a single sheet.
One sentence had been written in precise black ink:
The subject believes he is awake. Investigation ongoing.
Then I awoke.
Again.
Morning light filtered through the curtains. The room appeared ordinary. The clock was silent.
Yet on the bedside table rested a black file.
And somewhere beneath the waking city, almost too faint to hear, came the distant sound of typewriters, followed by the howl of a wolf.

Visit in Berlin

Last week, 6 9 N & F visited Berlin's Con-Area Studio – and used the time productively: From our material recorded on May 31, 2026, two remarkable reworks were created, transforming the original material into new sonic landscapes.

The musician 6 9 N & F is the head of Klappstuhl Records and for decades one of the defining voices of the German industrial scene. With an extensive back catalog documented on Discogs, Klappstuhl Records has established a consistent and uncompromising sound over the years – somewhere between darkly pulsating industrial, electronic texture, and conceptual rigor.

That 6 9 N & F took the time to put our material through his remix filter is a real honor for us. Both mixes speak for themselves.

releases.title: A Passport to Magonia
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by D
releases.description:
He knew the hole was waiting.

His father had told him without telling him. Not with words the women could hear, not near the fire, not where the younger children slept. Only once, while sharpening bone beside the river, his father had touched his chest and then pointed toward the black mouth of the cave.

“You will go in as one thing,” he had said. “You will come out with another name.”

So when the day came, the boy did not resist.

The men painted his face with ash and red earth. His mother placed her hand on his head, then turned away. No one sang. No one smiled. The old ones led him past the fire at the entrance, past the first animals on the stone, past the places where children were allowed to look.

The cave narrowed.

The light behind him became a smell.

Smoke, fat, wet rock, old breath.

At the far end, where the fire could no longer reach, the elder pointed upward. There was the gap: a wound in the stone, barely wide enough for a body that had not yet become a man.

The boy climbed.

The rock held his ribs. It pressed his knees. His eyes tried to drink the last crumbs of light, but there were none. He moved by scraping, by smelling, by remembering his father’s silence.

Then the stone opened.

He fell forward into a chamber no child had ever named.

At first there was nothing.

Then the torch entered behind him.

The walls woke.

Hands. Animals. Long bodies with heads like suns. Hunters smaller than insects. Beasts he knew, and others that should not have had bodies at all. Figures rose from cracks in the stone as if the cave had dreamed them before men learned to speak.

The boy trembled.

An elder took his hand and pressed it against the wall.

Another lifted the torch and began to walk.

And as the shadows moved, the figures began their story.

releases.title: Half a House
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
The house was exactly half a house.
Not destroyed. Not unfinished.
A clean cut divided it from roof to cellar, exposing wallpaper, beds, pipes, and a staircase ascending into open sky.
I rented it because it was cheaper than certainty.
The landlord arrived wearing a suit made of maps.
"Do not enter the missing half," he said.
"There is no missing half."
He looked at me with pity.
"That is what it wants you to think."
Each morning the house rearranged itself.
The bedroom migrated into the kitchen. The kitchen drifted into a corridor filled with umbrellas. A clock grew from the ceiling and produced eggs instead of hours.
The exposed wall breathed slowly.
Sometimes it whispered.
I could never remember what it said.
Letters arrived daily from the Bureau of Absent Structures.
The envelopes contained feathers, fish scales, and tiny photographs of rooms I had never seen.
One image showed me sleeping.
Another showed me standing beside a woman whose head was a burning rose.
On the seventh day I discovered a door hanging in empty air where the missing half should have been.
Beyond it stretched a railway station.
Trains arrived carrying suitcases full of rain.
Passengers wore masks shaped like houses.
No one spoke.
A conductor punched holes in the sky.
I followed him through a tunnel lined with typewriters. The machines typed continuously without paper.
The words emerged directly into the air:

HALF A HOUSE.
HALF A MAN.
HALF A MEMORY.

The tunnel opened into a vast room.
There stood the missing half of the house.
It floated above a black sea.
Its windows blinked like eyes.
Its chimney exhaled birds.
I felt an immediate homesickness for a place I had never lived.
A woman emerged from the front door.
Her face resembled my own.
"You took the wrong half," she said.
"What does that mean?"
"You were divided with the house."
The sea rose.
The room folded inward like a closing flower.
Suddenly I was back in my bed.
Morning.
The exposed wall glowed in pale sunlight.
Everything appeared normal.
Except that half my reflection was gone.
Days later the landlord returned.
He examined me carefully and nodded.
"The transfer is nearly complete."
"What transfer?"
But he was already leaving.
That night I heard footsteps moving through invisible rooms.
Laughter drifted from the absent staircase.
Someone switched on a light in the missing half.
At dawn the house disappeared.
Only the cut remained, standing upright in the landscape like a monument.
People ask me where I live now.
I point to the empty space beside me.
Most see nothing.
Sometimes, however, they step aside politely, as if making room for a house that is still arriving.

releases.title: The trade wind desert soundscape
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced By K
releases.description:
The trade wind desert soundscape

Vastness begins —
the overwhelming weight of soundlessness,
until the heat itself starts to hum.
A low-frequency vibration you feel in your chest,
than you hear it inside your skull.

The day is a sharp orchestra:
the grating grit underfoot,
a perpetual shhh where the sand shifts and slides,
carrying tiny pebbles like gossip.

When the thermal currents rise, they distort the air —
a visible sound, shimmering waves of heat,
that roar without noise,
an immense pressure building in your ears.

Then comes the night. The silence is frozen;
it’s packed tight with expectation.
The uniquely flat head of a sand snake cutting through the velvet dark.
The distant, raw lament of a Fennec Fox,
a sound that travels forever

It is the constant conversation between wind, stone, and time—
a wild breathing. 
that never resolves, only repeats,
infinite and demanding.

releases.title: 41ºC and My Brain Feels Like It's Boiling
releases.creator: MDK
releases.credits: Produced by M
releases.description:
Berlin has always been a city that prefers complaints to miracles. If the sun shines, it is too bright. If it rains, it is too wet. This year the weather solved the argument by becoming both an inconvenience and a public spectacle.

Forty-one degrees.

The number appeared on every screen like a government decree. It was too hot to think, too hot to argue, and certainly too hot to pretend that climate was just another topic for polite conversation.

The pavements softened. The tram tracks glittered like they were considering retirement. Even the pigeons had abandoned politics and occupied every fountain with admirable discipline.

The cafés were full of people drinking lukewarm iced coffee. Berlin has a remarkable talent for making cold things arrive warm. Nobody seemed surprised. Disappointment is part of the local architecture.

At the office, shirts surrendered before noon. Ties disappeared like unpopular ministers. Meetings became competitions in silent endurance. Someone suggested opening the windows. Someone else reminded him that outside was an oven with bicycles.

The tourists remained cheerful. They photographed graffiti, construction sites, and sweating Berliners with the same fascination usually reserved for ancient ruins. They called it authentic. The locals called it Tuesday.

Meanwhile the politicians discovered trees. Shade suddenly became a campaign promise. Every speech contained the word *future*. Every street contained very little shade.

By late afternoon the city moved in slow motion. Cyclists rolled downhill whenever possible. Dogs negotiated shorter walks. Street musicians played jazz so slowly that it sounded like classical music melting.

And yet Berlin refused to surrender.

There is something peculiar about this city. It survives not through optimism but through sarcasm. Hope is considered suspicious; irony is reliable. When life becomes unbearable, Berlin simply invents better jokes.

Then one evening clouds gathered. Thunder rolled across the rooftops. The first raindrop landed on the overheated pavement with the applause of an exhausted audience.

People smiled for exactly three seconds.

Then someone muttered, "Typical Berlin. Now it's too humid."

The city had returned to normal.
